title: React Rendering and Reconciliation
summary: How React's reconciler diffs the virtual DOM, what triggers re-renders, keys, React.memo, and performance mental models.
guide:
# React Rendering and Reconciliation ## The Pipeline: From setState to pixels ``` setState() / new props │ ▼ React marks component as 'dirty' │ ▼ Render phase (pure, can be interrupted) component() returns new JSX tree │ ▼ Reconciliation — diff new tree vs old tree 'What changed since last time?' │ ▼ Commit phase (synchronous, not interruptible) Apply only the CHANGED DOM operations │ ▼ Browser paints new pixels │ ▼ useEffect / useLayoutEffect run ``` ## Virtual DOM — the diff algorithm React doesn't compare every node in the tree exhaustively (that would be O(n³)). It uses heuristics to get O(n): ``` Heuristic 1: Different element types = replace the whole subtree Old:
text
New:

text

→ div becomes p: React unmounts div subtree, mounts p subtree (Even though inside is identical) Heuristic 2: Same element type = update attributes, recurse into children Old:
hello
New:
hello
→ Update className attribute only (no unmount) Heuristic 3: Keys — stable child identity Old: [, , ] New: [, ] ← b removed React matches by key → removes only b Without keys: React doesn't know which item was removed → might update wrong items or unmount everything ``` ## What triggers a re-render? ``` ┌─────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Trigger │ Affected components │ ├─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤ │ setState / dispatch │ The component + descendants │ │ Props change │ The component + descendants │ │ Context value change│ All useContext() consumers │ │ Parent re-renders │ All children (by default) │ │ forceUpdate (class) │ The component │ └─────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────┘ Critical insight: When Parent re-renders, ALL its children re-render — even if their props haven't changed — UNLESS they are wrapped in React.memo. ``` ## React.memo — skipping renders from parent ```tsx // Without memo: re-renders every time Parent re-renders function ExpensiveChild({ data }: { data: string[] }) { // Expensive rendering logic } // With memo: only re-renders when data prop changes (by reference) const ExpensiveChild = React.memo(function ExpensiveChild({ data }) { // Expensive rendering logic }); // Custom comparator: when deep comparison is needed const Child = React.memo( ({ config }) =>
{config.title}
, (prevProps, nextProps) => prevProps.config.id === nextProps.config.id // return true = skip re-render // return false = re-render ); ``` ``` Parent renders: Without memo: Child always re-renders With memo: Child compares prev/next props with Object.is Same reference? → SKIP Different reference? → RE-RENDER Common trap: passing new object/array expressions as props always creates new references — memo always re-renders. Fix with useMemo or useCallback. ``` ## Keys — identity for list items ```tsx // BAD: using index as key {items.map((item, index) => ( ))} // Problem: if items are reordered, React sees key=0 still present // and updates it with new data — triggering unnecessary work // WORSE: if Item has internal state (e.g., an input), the wrong // state sticks to the wrong item after reorder // GOOD: use stable unique ID {items.map(item => ( ))} // React correctly matches each key to its previous instance ``` ``` Key forces unmount+remount: → unmount with key="a" → mount fresh with key="b" Useful pattern: reset a component's state by changing its key // When userId changes, key changes → form resets automatically ``` ## The Render and Commit Phases ``` Render phase (pure, can be interrupted in Concurrent React): - React calls your component function - Your function returns JSX describing what SHOULD be - React builds new virtual DOM tree - React diffs new vs old → No side effects here! Render can be called multiple times Commit phase (synchronous, cannot interrupt): - React applies changes to the real DOM - React reads layout effects (useLayoutEffect) - Browser paints - React runs passive effects (useEffect) ``` ## Why renders don't always become DOM updates ``` Render = React calling your component function ≠ DOM update = React actually changing the browser DOM Timeline: Parent renders → Parent's JSX has

questions.prompt: What does a React 'render' actually mean?
choices: React updating the browser DOM
React calling your component function to produce a new JSX description of what the UI should look like
React painting pixels to the screen
The browser's paint cycle
questions.explanation: A 'render' is React calling your component function. The output is a JavaScript object tree (virtual DOM). React may then diff this against the previous output. A render does NOT mean the DOM changes — if nothing in the diff changed, no DOM operations run.

questions.prompt: By default, when a parent component re-renders, its children:
choices: Only re-render if their props changed
Never re-render unless explicitly told to
Always re-render — React re-renders all descendants by default
Re-render in background threads
questions.explanation: Without React.memo, every child re-renders when the parent does — regardless of prop changes. This is usually fine because renders are cheap. But for expensive trees, React.memo can skip the child's render when its props haven't changed.

questions.prompt: React's tree diffing algorithm achieves O(n) complexity (instead of O(n³)) by:
choices: Using WebAssembly
Assuming elements of different types produce different trees (replace whole subtree), and using keys to identify list items
Comparing only leaf nodes
Running diffs in parallel threads
questions.explanation: React's heuristics: 1) Different element type = unmount and remount (no deep diff needed). 2) Same element type = update in place and recurse. 3) Keys identify stable children in lists. These three rules let React diff any tree in a single pass.

questions.prompt: Why is using index as a list key problematic when items can be reordered?
choices: Indexes are slower to compare than strings
When items reorder, key=0 maps to a different item — React updates in place instead of moving, causing wrong state to stick to the wrong item
React disallows numeric keys
Index keys prevent useMemo from working
questions.explanation: If you have [A, B, C] with keys [0, 1, 2] and reorder to [C, A, B], keys become [0, 1, 2] again — React sees key=0 still present and updates it with C's data. If A had an open dropdown, that dropdown is now opened on the wrong component. Use stable IDs.

questions.prompt: React.memo prevents re-renders from:
choices: All sources — including state, context, and parent renders
Parent re-renders only — state and context changes still re-render memo'd components
Only context changes
Only state changes internal to the component
questions.explanation: React.memo compares PROPS shallowly. If props haven't changed, it skips the render triggered by the parent. But if the component's own state changes or it consumes a changed context, React.memo cannot help — those are direct triggers.

questions.prompt: A component is wrapped in React.memo. Its parent re-renders. Will it re-render if this is its prop?
choices: No — React.memo sees 'config' is the same label
Yes — `{ title: 'Dashboard' }` is a new object reference every render; Object.is fails; React.memo re-renders
Only on the first render
Depends on React version
questions.explanation: `{ title: 'Dashboard' }` in JSX creates a new JavaScript object on every render. Object.is comparison: `{} !== {}` (different references). React.memo sees 'config changed' and re-renders MemoChild — even though the content is logically identical. Fix: useMemo or define the config outside the component.

questions.prompt: What happens when you change a key on a component?
choices: React updates the component's props
React unmounts the old component instance and mounts a fresh one — all state is reset
React moves the component in the DOM tree
Nothing — keys only affect lists
questions.explanation: Key is React's identity signal. When a key changes, React treats it as a completely different element — unmounting the old instance (running cleanup effects) and mounting a fresh one (no previous state). This is a deliberate pattern to reset component state: `<Form key={userId} />`.

questions.prompt: Which cause a React component to re-render?
choices: Its parent re-renders (without memo)
A useContext() context value it reads changes
A ref.current mutation
Its own useState setter is called with a new value
questions.explanation: Parent renders (A), context changes (B), and own state changes (D) all trigger re-renders. Ref mutations (C) do NOT trigger renders — that's the entire point of useRef.

questions.prompt: The Render phase in Concurrent React differs from the Commit phase in that:
choices: Render runs on the server; Commit runs on the client
Render is pure and interruptible (React can pause and restart); Commit is synchronous and must complete — it applies DOM changes
Render reads state; Commit writes state
They run in parallel
questions.explanation: In Concurrent Mode, React can start a render, pause it to handle urgent updates, then resume or discard. This is why your component function must be pure — it may be called multiple times for a single commit. The Commit phase applies DOM changes — it's always synchronous and can't be interrupted.

questions.prompt: If React renders a component and the new JSX is structurally identical to the old one, what DOM work happens?
choices: Full DOM replacement
No DOM operations — React's differ sees no changes and skips the commit
React still writes to DOM for safety
Only text nodes are checked
questions.explanation: Renders and DOM updates are separate steps. If the reconciler diffs the new virtual DOM tree against the old one and finds zero differences, the commit phase has no DOM mutations to make. The component ran but the browser DOM wasn't touched.

questions.prompt: React's heuristic: when it encounters `<div>` where `<span>` was previously, it:
choices: Converts span to div in place
Unmounts the entire span subtree and mounts the div subtree fresh — even if children are identical
Keeps children, only changes the tag
Throws a warning but continues
questions.explanation: Different element types = completely different tree. React doesn't try to reuse children across type changes — it unmounts the old subtree (running cleanup effects) and mounts the new one. This is why small type changes can be expensive in large trees.

questions.prompt: `startTransition(() => setResults(heavy))` tells React:
choices: Run this update in a web worker
This update is non-urgent — can be interrupted and deferred if a more urgent update (like a keystroke) arrives
Batch all setState calls inside
Delay the update by a fixed timeout
questions.explanation: `startTransition` marks state updates as 'non-urgent transitions'. If the user types while the transition renders, React pauses the transition render, handles the urgent keystroke, then resumes or restarts the transition. This keeps the input responsive during expensive filtering.

questions.prompt: A memo'd component with props `{ items: [] }` still re-renders every parent render. Why?
choices: Arrays are never memoized in React
`[]` is a new array reference on every render — Object.is([], []) is false; memo comparison fails every time
Lists require keys to be memoized
useMemo is needed for non-primitive state
questions.explanation: `[]` in JSX creates a new JavaScript array object on every render. Even though it's empty, it's a different reference each time. Object.is([], []) === false. React.memo sees 'items changed' and re-renders. Fix: `const emptyArr = useMemo(() => [], [])` or define outside the component.

questions.prompt: React DevTools Profiler shows a component rendered with reason 'parent rendered'. The fix is:
choices: Add more useEffect deps
Wrap the component in React.memo — if its props are stable, it will skip renders from parent
Move the component to a separate file
Use a key prop
questions.explanation: 'Parent rendered' in the Profiler means the component re-rendered purely because its parent did — not because its own state or props changed. React.memo wraps the component: if the props are shallowly equal to last render, the component is skipped.

questions.prompt: What does this intentional key change do?
choices: Prevents ProfileForm from rendering
Each time userId changes, React unmounts old ProfileForm and mounts fresh — all form state resets
Shares state between ProfileForm instances
key has no effect on non-list components
questions.explanation: This is the intentional key-reset pattern. When userId changes, key changes — React destroys the old ProfileForm instance (runs cleanup effects, discards all useState) and creates a fresh one. This resets all internal form state without needing explicit reset logic in the component.

questions.prompt: After a render, when do useEffect callbacks run?
choices: Before the DOM is updated (synchronously in render phase)
After the component renders AND after the browser has painted
Before the browser paints but after DOM update
They don't run after re-renders, only on mount
questions.explanation: useEffect is a 'passive effect' — it fires after the browser has painted the new frame. This means the user sees the update before the effect runs. This is the right timing for data fetching, subscriptions, and non-visual side effects.

questions.prompt: Which strategies prevent expensive child re-renders?
choices: React.memo on the child
useMemo on object/array props passed to the child
useCallback on function props passed to the child
Adding the child to a dep array
questions.explanation: React.memo (A) skips renders when props are shallowly equal. useMemo (B) stabilises object/array references. useCallback (C) stabilises function references. All three together make memo work correctly for all prop types. Dep arrays (D) are unrelated to rendering prevention.

questions.prompt: React's fiber architecture allows:
choices: Running components as native threads
Interruptible, incremental rendering — React can pause work, handle higher-priority updates, then resume or restart
Rendering to canvas instead of DOM
Sharing state across browser tabs
questions.explanation: Fiber is React's internal reconciler. Each component is a 'fiber' unit of work. Concurrent React can pause the render of low-priority fibers, process high-priority updates (like user input), then resume. This is what makes useTransition and Suspense work.

questions.prompt: When does a React render NOT result in any DOM changes?
choices: When the component is inside React.memo
When React's reconciler diffs new vs old virtual DOM and finds no differences — the commit phase is a no-op
When the component is inside a Context Provider
Renders always result in DOM changes
questions.explanation: Render = React calling your function. DOM update = React making changes to the browser DOM. These are separate. If the reconciler sees no differences between old and new virtual DOM, the commit phase produces zero DOM operations. The render 'happened' but was effectively free from the user's perspective.

questions.prompt: The best time to reach for React.memo is:
choices: Wrap every component by default
After measuring with the Profiler that a component re-renders frequently and the render is measurably slow or causes visible jank
When using TypeScript
For all components with more than 3 props
questions.explanation: React.memo has a cost — it stores props, runs comparisons. For cheap components, the comparison can cost more than the render. Profile first. Add memo when you see specific components rendering unnecessarily and see a measurable improvement in the Profiler.

flashcards.back: Render = React calling your component function. DOM update = React writing to the browser DOM. The reconciler diffs render output — if nothing changed, zero DOM operations happen.
Shallow Object.is comparison of props. New object/array/function = new reference = memo re-renders. Pair with useMemo/useCallback to stabilise references.
Same key = update in place. Changed key = unmount old, mount fresh (state reset). Useful pattern: `key={userId}` to reset a form when user changes.

flashcards.front: List key — use ID, not index
flashcards.back: Index keys break when items reorder — React attaches state to the wrong item. Use stable unique IDs. Index ok only for static, never-reordered lists.
React.memo does NOT block context-triggered re-renders. It only blocks parent-triggered renders. Split contexts to reduce consumer blast radius.
Render: pure, interruptible, can run multiple times. Commit: synchronous, applies DOM changes, then runs layout effects. Side effects belong in commit (useEffect).
`startTransition(() => expensiveSetState(...))` marks as deferrable. Urgent updates (keystrokes) interrupt it. `isPending` flag shows progress. Keeps UI responsive.
Own state change, context change, parent re-render. NOT: ref mutation, memoized value with same deps, calling a function. Profile to find which trigger is firing.

project.brief: You have a dashboard with a `<DataTable items={1000 items} />` component that re-renders every second because a parent timer updates a clock display. Profile (mentally or with DevTools) and design an optimisation strategy: 1) What tools tell you this component is the bottleneck? 2) What is the minimum change to stop unnecessary renders? 3) The items list is computed from a filter function — where do you memoize? 4) The table has a delete handler `onDelete(id)` — how do you stabilise it? 5) What is the risk of over-memoizing this component?

checklist.text: Identifies React DevTools Profiler → 'parent rendered' reason on DataTable
Wraps DataTable in React.memo as minimum fix
Memoizes filtered items: const filtered = useMemo(() => filter(items, query), [items, query])
Stabilises onDelete with useCallback: const onDelete = useCallback(id => ..., [relevant deps])
Explains risk: if items or onDelete still create new references each render, memo comparison fails and renders continue anyway

hints: React DevTools Profiler: record, click, stop — hover each bar to see why it rendered.
React.memo on DataTable: `export default React.memo(DataTable)` — only re-renders when items/onDelete props change.
useMemo for filtered list: put the filter logic in useMemo with [rawItems, query] deps.
useCallback for onDelete: needs the same deps as the delete logic — if it reads state, add that state to deps.
